fuzzy_prd:
hey team, filing this based on some feedback we got from a few backend devs who are trying to wire up our log shipping pipeline. basically the complaint is that every team is rolling their own version of the same plumbing — someone manually builds the JSON body, someone else wrote their own retry loop, another person has their own timestamp hack to avoid duplicate entries. it's a mess and nothing is consistent.

we need a library/adapter thing that handles all of this in one place. it should be able to take log events and pack them into the right wire format for the ingest endpoint (we use that same push API as before, similar to what the metrics team did with their protobuf stuff a few months back). it also needs to group events by their label identity — some events come in with extra labels attached at emit time that should affect grouping. timestamps need to be deduplicated somehow within the same millisecond bucket.

on the delivery side, when something fails it should retry automatically — but there's a special case for rate-limit responses that the PM from infra mentioned should be configurable. also there's a buffering layer that should respect a byte ceiling, and reuse allocated buffers when possible.

label text can come in different formats with different separators — including regex-based ones — and bad label data should surface as a clean error, not a stack trace. there's also some string utility stuff we apparently need for encoding correctness. let's get this scoped and built.

one extra pass on the details people asked about: for the JSON push payloads, the timestamp we send is the nanosecond timestamp string computed as (timestamp_ms * 1_000_000) + nanos. so for example, timestamp_ms=3000 and nanos=1 produces the string "3000000001". that’s the exact shape we should be emitting there. also on the duplicate timestamp issue, for repeated events within the same millisecond, the nanosecond suffix is incremented by 1 for each successive event in that millisecond. the first event at timestamp_ms=X gets suffix (X % 1000) * 1000, the second gets that plus 1, and so on. if a timestamp older than the current maximum is seen for a given millisecond, it is clamped to the last used suffix for that millisecond. if more than 1000 events occur within the same millisecond, the suffix is capped at the maximum (999 offset) and subsequent events reuse it.

also to be really clear on grouping: records are grouped into streams based on their full label set. two records are placed in the same stream only if their label keys and values are identical. per-event marker labels are appended to the base label identity, so any difference in marker labels causes those records to be placed in separate streams. same idea for Per-event marker_labels maps (Feature 2.3): additional label key/value pairs supplied per log event that are appended to the stream identity, causing records with differing marker labels to be placed in separate streams, as defined in feature2_3_marker_labels.json.

for message content, the actual text should stay the same, but when it gets embedded in JSON push payloads it must be JSON-escaped. carriage returns become \r, line feeds become \n, tabs become \t, and other control characters are similarly escaped. the original content must not be altered — only the JSON encoding representation changes. that’s just so the ingest API always gets valid JSON no matter what characters showed up in the log message. and for the rendered line format we’ve been using in batch and HTTP delivery checks, it is exactly "l=<LEVEL> c=<logger> t=<thread> | <message> " with the trailing space. for example, an event with level=INFO, logger=test.TestApp, thread=thread-1, message="Test message 1" renders as "l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-1 | Test message 1 ".

one more batching edge case: when a single event's encoded size exceeds the configured batch_max_bytes limit, that individual event is silently dropped. the remaining valid events in the input sequence continue to be batched and sent normally. the oversized event does not cause the batch to be rejected or the adapter to halt — it is simply excluded from the output payload.

on retries, the rate-limit behavior is driven by the configurable flag called drop_rate_limited. when drop_rate_limited=false, rate-limited failures are retried the same as connection failures up to the configured retry count. the test contract shows 3 send attempts for both connection failures and rate-limited failures when drop_rate_limited=false. when drop_rate_limited=true, the batch should be dropped without retrying.

and yes, this scope also includes Feature 1.2 protobuf_batch operation: serializing log records into a Snappy-compressed Protobuf binary push payload matching the Loki push API binary wire format, as defined in feature1_2_protobuf_push_payload.json.

trigger_keywords: how should malformed label text be reported
what error format is used for invalid label pairs
what happens if a label segment cannot be split into key and value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a label segment cannot be split into exactly one key and one value using the configured separators, the system must emit a normalized domain error with the exact format: "error=invalid_label_pair\nrendered_labels=<the raw rendered label text>\n". No host-language exception class names or runtime stack information should appear in the output.

trigger_keywords: how does the regex pair separator work
what is the format for specifying a regex-based pair separator
how are comment lines handled in label patterns
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A regex-based pair separator is specified by prefixing the separator string with "regex:". For example, "regex:(\n|//[^\n]+)+" will split label text on any combination of newlines and comment lines (lines starting with //). Empty segments produced after splitting are ignored and not parsed as label pairs.

trigger_keywords: how does the byte queue capacity work
what happens when an offered batch would exceed the byte limit
when is capacity released in the byte queue
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The byte queue tracks total queued bytes against a configured max_bytes ceiling. An offered batch is accepted only if its byte size plus the currently queued bytes does not exceed max_bytes. If it would exceed the limit, the batch is rejected (accepted=false) and queued_bytes remains unchanged. Capacity is released when a batch is borrowed from the queue — not when it is returned. After borrowing, the queued_bytes decreases by the size of the borrowed batch.

trigger_keywords: how does buffer reuse work in the byte queue
what is the pool in the byte queue used for
when is a pooled buffer reused for a new batch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The byte queue maintains an internal pool of reusable byte buffers. When a borrowed batch buffer is returned (via a return/recycle operation), it is added to the pool. When a new batch is offered and a pooled buffer of compatible capacity exists, the pooled buffer is reused rather than allocating a new one. The pool_size line in test output reflects how many buffers are currently in the pool at that point in the operation sequence.

trigger_keywords: how is the protobuf payload verified or decoded in tests
what is the text output format used when validating binary push payloads
how should the binary payload be represented in the test adapter output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For protobuf/binary push payloads, the test adapter must decode the compressed binary payload back into a neutral text representation. The format is: a "stream={<label_key>="<label_value>",...}" line per stream, followed by "timestamp=<ms>.<nanos> line=<message>" lines for each record in that stream. The on-heap vs direct_buffer flag must not affect the decoded content — both must produce identical output.

trigger_keywords: what is the HTTP endpoint path for the push request
what header is used for multi-tenant routing
what HTTP status code indicates a successful push
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The push endpoint path is "/loki/api/v1/push". Multi-tenant routing uses the header key "X-Scope-OrgID" (or equivalent tenant header) with the tenant identifier value. A successful delivery is indicated by HTTP status 204 (No Content).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases

what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: there's a special case for rate-limit responses that the PM from infra mentioned should be configurable
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The drop_rate_limited boolean flag in the retry policy (Feature 5.2): controls whether HTTP 429/rate-limited responses cause the batch to be dropped instead of retried, as defined in feature5_2_retry_policy.json

The byte queue buffer pool (Feature 6.2): an internal pool that holds returned ByteBuffer instances so they can be reused for subsequent batch offers instead of allocating new memory, as defined in feature6_2_byte_queue_reuse.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: there's also some string utility stuff we apparently need for encoding correctness
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7.1 string utilities: UTF-8 byte length calculation for strings (including multi-byte unicode characters) and blank-string classification (null, empty, or whitespace-only), as defined in feature7_1_string_utilities.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from the current ticket. You'll need to look at the existing codebase patterns or check with the engineering lead — I only have the high-level business context captured here.

feature1_1_json_push_payload.json.description: Serializes log records into the JSON push wire format, grouping records by equal label sets and preserving each record timestamp and line.

cases.expected_output: {"streams":[{"stream":{"level":"DEBUG","app":"my-app"},"values":[["3000000001","l=DEBUG c=test.TestApp t=thread-2 | Test message 2"]]},{"stream":{"level":"INFO","app":"my-app"},"values":[["1000000002","l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-1 | Test message 1"],["2000000003","l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-3 | Test message 4"],["5000000004","l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-1 | Test message 3"]]}]}
{"streams":[{"stream":{"level":"INFO","app":"my-app"},"values":[["100000000","l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-1 | Test message"]]}]}

feature1_2_protobuf_push_payload.json.description: Serializes log records into the compressed binary push format; when decoded, the payload contains the same stream labels, timestamps, and lines.

feature2_1_label_pair_parsing.json.description: Parses rendered label text into ordered key/value fields, honoring configured separators, regex separators, and empty segments.

cases.expected_output:
label=level value=INFO
label=app value="my"app
label=test value=test

label=level value=INFO
label=app value="my"app
label=test value=test

label=level value=INFO
label=app value="my"app

feature2_2_invalid_label_pair_errors.jso.description: Reports malformed rendered label text as a normalized label-pair error without exposing host-language exception names.

cases.expected_output:
error=invalid_label_pair
rendered_labels=level=INFO,app="my"app,test=test

feature2_3_marker_labels.json.description: Adds per-event marker label fields to the rendered stream so records with different marker labels are grouped separately.

cases.expected_output:
stream=[l, INFO, mrk, mrk-val]
record=ts=103 INFO | Test message 2
stream=[l, INFO, stcmrk, stat-val]
record=ts=100 INFO | Test message 1
record=ts=105 INFO | Test message 3
stream=[l, INFO, mrk1, v1, mrk2, v2]
record=ts=104 INFO | Test message 4

feature3_1_timestamp_nanos.json.description: Assigns a nanosecond suffix inside each millisecond to preserve event ordering and clamps older or overflowed events to the last suffix for that millisecond.

cases.expected_output:
timestamp_ms=1123 nanos=123000
timestamp_ms=1123 nanos=123001
timestamp_ms=1123 nanos=123002
timestamp_ms=1123 nanos=123003
timestamp_ms=1122 nanos=122999
timestamp_ms=1124 nanos=124000

feature4_1_batch_size_flush.json.description: Buffers appended log events until the configured item threshold is reached, then sends one grouped payload.

cases.expected_output:
sent_before_threshold=false
stream=[level, INFO, app, my-app]
record=ts=100 l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-1 | Test message 1 
record=ts=107 l=INFO c=test.TestApp t=thread-1 | Test message 3 
stream=[level, WARN, app, my-app]
record=ts=104 l=WARN c=test.TestApp t=thread-2 | Test message 2

feature4_2_batch_ordering.json.description: Controls whether records are emitted in arrival order or sorted by timestamp, and whether labels are evaluated once for the batch or per event.

record=ts=105 INFO | Test message 1
record=ts=103 DEBUG | Test message 2
record=ts=100 INFO | Test message 3

feature6_1_byte_queue_capacity.json.description: Accepts binary batches while the total queued bytes stay within the configured limit, rejects batches that would exceed it, and releases capacity when a batch is borrowed.

cases.expected_output:

offer batch_id=1 accepted=true queued_bytes=8

borrow batch_id=1 items=1 bytes=4 data=[4,5,6,7] queued_bytes=4
borrow batch_id=2 items=1 bytes=4 data=[8,9,10,11] queued_bytes=0

pool_size=1

pool_size=0
